Note: We have glimpses into the lives of enslaved people held by the long-time curé of this parish, Father Guilaume Silvestre Delahaye, present in the parish at least since the mid 1770s, based on the interrogation of him carried out by Civil Commissioner Sonthonax on Feb. 1, 1793 (AN D/XXV/5, Dossier 53, Pièce 33). Delahaye was among the priests who accompanied some of the insurgents of the Northern Plains Uprising. He makes several mentions of a "mulâtresse," among his slaves, a close confidant. He also mentions that his plantation was not spared by the insurgents.  A "codicil" to Delahaye's will, submitted upon his return in late 1792, provides for the manumission of three "mulâtresses," firstly, Françoise, then Catherine and Florence (AN D/XXV/5, Dossier 49, Pièce 19).

Reference: Clark, Emily. Masterless Mistresses: The New Orleans Ursulines and the Development of a New World Society, 1727–1834. Williamsburg, VA: Omohundro Institute of Early American History and Culture; Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2007, 169. Pastor, Lori Renee. "Black Catholicism: Religion and Slavery in Antebellum Louisiana." PhD diss., Louisiana State University and Agricultural and Mechanical College, 2005, 58.
Note: All the major Catholic communities in greater New Orleans possessed slaves, including the Jesuits before their expulsion in the middle of the eighteenth century. In 1770, the plantation operated by the French, and French- and Spanish-Creole Ursuline nuns of New Orleans was home to 61 slaves, and in 1791, 17 slaves were recorded as living in the convent itself (24 in 1795). By the turn of the nineteenth century, the Ursuline and Capuchin establishments collectively claimed 123 enslaved dependents. This figure for the Capuchins is an estimate based on the number of reported enslaved individuals residing on two Ursuline properties (the plantation, 61; the convent, 24) in the late 18th century, according to Emily Clark's research.

"État du Clergé du département de la Marine en 1782." Service historique de la Défense, Fonds de la bibliothèque de la Marine, G191, pp. 397–408. Office de Tourisme de la Guadeloupe. "Église Saint-Joseph (Vieux-Habitants)." https://fr.guadeloupe-tourisme.com/623/Eglise-saintjoseph-vieuxhabitants/.
Note: The original church, founded by Dominican fathers Breton and Du Tertre, dates back to 1636. Initially built of wood, it was gradually improved but was destroyed by fire during the English landing on March 20, 1703. It was reconstructed in stone by the Capuchins, under the guidance of Father Vincent, in the same year and expanded in 1721. The Capuchins, who had secured permanent settlement rights in Guadeloupe in 1681, were entrusted with Vieux-Habitants. Their coat of arms adorns the front of the portal. After the Jesuit church of Carmel in Basse-Terre, this is the oldest church in Guadeloupe. Inside, there is a commemorative chapel dedicated to the founding fathers. The church houses a collection of religious silverware, mostly from the mid-18th century (processional crosses, sanctuary lamps, a ciborium, with a cup engraved with a depiction of the marriage of the Virgin Mary and Saint Joseph). The commune of Vieux-Habitants plans to create an eco-museum to display these artifacts. Until then, visitors can view these silver pieces at the departmental archives of Guadeloupe in Gourbeyre. The author of the "Etat du Clerge" report (1782) does not record how many enslaved persons served the parish.

Corps-Officiel: Ordre des Frères de Notre-Dame du Mont Carmel
Province: Order of the Brothers of the Blessed Virgin Mary of Mount Carmel
Reference: "État des biens et revenus des Carmes, dressé en (1741, 1743)." Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer, F5/A/7/3. Aix-en-Provence. Dussert-Vidalet, Bernadette. "L'habitation du Mont-Carmel." Bulletin de la Société d'Histoire de la Guadeloupe, no. 32 (1977): 5–23.
Note: According to a declaration made in 1743, the Carmelite fathers served seven parishes in the French Antilles (3 on Guadeloupe, 1 in Les Saintes, 3 on Marie-Galante). The central Carmelite Convent, located in Guadeloupe, provided each parish with an average of three enslaved workers to serve the priests and maintain church buildings and grounds. The Carmelites also  possessed two sugar plantations on Guadeloupe to support their missions, the oldest at Montagne de Beau Soleil (known as “Mont Carmel”) and another at Belle-Vue, both near the town of Basse-Terre. The Mont Carmel plantation counted 57 enslaved dependents in 1772, predominantly of Igbo descent (but also Bambara, Arada, and Congo).

The central Carmelite Convent, located in Guadeloupe, provided each parish with an average of three enslaved workers to serve the priests and maintain church buildings and grounds. The Carmelites also  possessed two sugar plantations on Guadeloupe to support their missions, the oldest at Montagne de Beau Soleil (known as “Mont Carmel”) and another at Belle-Vue, both near the town of Basse-Terre. Reference: "Rolle général des habitants de l'isle de Sainte Croix avec le dénombrement de leurs femmes et enfans par compagnies" (April 16, 1681). Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer, G1/498, fol. 70. Jan, Jean-Marie. Les congrégations religieuses au Cap Français, Saint-Domingue, 1681–1793. Port-au-Prince: H. Deschamps, 1951, 9.
Note: According to a 1681 census of the island of Sainte-Croix (now Saint Croix in the U.S. Virgin Islands), slaveholding Dominicans were among the earliest residents. During this period, the island was under French colonial control (1650–1733) and played a key role in the Caribbean's plantation economy, particularly in sugar production. After being sold to Denmark, Sainte-Croix later became part of the U.S. Virgin Islands in 1917. The Dominicans ran an early mission station on the island of Sainte Croix. The French colony there was evacuated to Saint Domingue at the end of the seventeenth century amidst war with Great Britain and the Netherlands.

Reference: "État du Clergé du département de la Marine en 1782." Service historique de la Défense, Fonds de la bibliothèque de la Marine, G191, p. 401.
Note: "Les Dominicains desservent les paroisses de cette colonie. Elles sont sous l’inspection du prefet de la Martinique[.] Je n’ai aucun renseignement a leur sujet. Je sai [sic] seulement qu’elles sont au nombre de six et elles doivent se multiplier en raison de l’etendue des défrichemens. Le Gouvernement ne s’est occupé de l’état spirituel de cette Isle que depuis le traité de paix de 1763.  On ignore si, depuis qu’elle est tombé entre les mains des Anglais Les missionnaires en ont été congédiés." (p. 401) The island was restored to France in 1783 (shortly after this report was authored).

Province: Dominicans of the Iles du Vent
Reference: "Transmission d'un état des biens des Dominicains à la Martinique par les administrateurs," June 12, 1785. Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer, F5 A 12. Aix-en-Provence.
Note: A 1784 report by the Dominicans documenting their expenses and revenues, includes a passage on the costs associated with maintaining Maison de St. Pierre, the urban headquarters of the Dominican mission, totaling 46,477 livres. This includes costs for clothing 59 enslaved servants who performed various crucial roles such as domestic chores, gardening, porter duties, personal assistance to residents, manual labor, support for religious activities, and specialized trades. These enslaved individuals would have played essential roles in the daily operations and upkeep of the mission headquarters under the supervision of its leadership.

Note: The Pères de la Charité owned land in Champflore (Le Morne-Rouge, Martinique) to support the maintenance of their hospital in Saint-Pierre (entrusted to them since 1685). They purchased a plantation there in 1777, and the property sale description included buildings, plantations of cassava, coffee, and cocoa, sugar manufacturing utensils, copper boilers, a rum boiler, 3 horses, 2 mares, 1 foal, 1 mule, 23 horned cattle, and a chapel for 120,000 livres. The plantation was purchased with 66 enslaved individuals, who would have joined those already owned by the Pères de la Charité. The Pères de la Charité retained its use after the French Revolution, although it became part of the national domain. Currently, only the manager's house remains from the original estate. After World War II, a Mr. de Meyac became the new owner, but in 1963, the S.A.F.E.R. (Société d'Aménagement Foncier et d'Établissement Rural) bought a large portion of the land for the former property workers.

Reference: Mémoire et consultation concernant un hôpital fondé par le feu sieur Staffort dans la paroisse des Cayes-du-Fond-de-l'Ile-à-Vache, en l'île S. Domingue. Cap-François: chez Marie, 1765.
Note: One Saint-Domingue planter (a naturalized Irishman) bequeathed all his property (and slaves) to go toward the foundation of a new hospital in Les Cayes, in the South. He expressly requested that the Freres not be involved in the management of the plantation property that would support their operations but to remain fully dedicated to caring for the sick.
Summary of Contract: In March 1719, Letters Patent were granted for two hospitals in Saint-Domingue to serve poor, sick, and injured men, providing medical treatment and necessary surgeries for their recovery, expanding the King's original intent for soldiers and sailors to include the poor. A dispute between the Brothers and Jesuits in Cap led to this solution. The Brothers received authorization to administer sacraments, conduct divine services, and bury the dead through priests of their choice, under the authority of the Provincial and Vicar General in France, recognizing their exemption from local ecclesiastical authorities based on Papal privileges, often leading to conflicts. The King granted them significant advantages, including ownership of churches, convents, hospitals, buildings, lands, and general property in Cap and Léogâne. The Provincial could change superiors at will, and the hospitals were under the King's special protection. The Brothers could slaughter animals for their own consumption, display France's arms on buildings, acquire property, receive donations, and collect alms in churches. They and their servants, both white and black, were exempt from guard duty, forced labor, and taxes for up to 50 servants in cultivation. They could import necessary goods from France without paying export or import duties, and free passage overseas was granted to the Brothers. In 1732, new Letters Patent reaffirmed the establishment and continuous progress of these hospitals.

Reference: Cabon, P. A. Notes sur l'histoire religieuse d'Haïti: de la Révolution au Concordat (1789–1860). Port-au-Prince: Petit Séminaire Collège Saint-Martial, 1933. Jan, Jean-Marie. Collecta. Vol. 1. Port-au-Prince: Editions Henri Deschamps, 1955, 85.
Note: An unknown number of slaves labored at the hospitals run by the Brothers of Charity in Saint-Domingue. The largest, La Providence, was located in the center of Cap Français. Toussaint Louverture himself was known to have once served the Brothers as a domestic. Another leader of the Northern Plains insurgency, Georges Biassou, was enslaved on their plantation outside the city. His mother, Diana, was among the first slaves freed from Church properties during the Revolution when Biassou led a daring rescue operation to liberate her and others enslaved at the Providence Hospital in January 1792, at the height of the standoff between the insurgent slaves and the colonial troops. During the War of American Independence, Saint-Ours, a lieutenant in the French Navy and commander of the Licorne, kept a journal detailing his stay in the region of Le Cap (Saint-Domingue), where he spent some time as the guest of the Pères de la Charité on their sugar plantation in Petite-Anse, outside of the city. In an entry from 25 September 1781, he describes the estate thus: "I saw the plan of the Fathers' estate, which comprised ninety-five carreaux of land in total. They had one hundred negroes and one hundred mules or horses to work it, producing about one hundred thousand pounds of sugar valued at 100,000 livres."

Note: An unknown number of slaves labored at the hospitals run by the Brothers of Charity in Saint-Domingue. The hospitals also possessed plantations to support their charitable operations. Toussaint Louverture himself was known to have once served the Brothers of Charity as a domestic. The order of Brothers Hospitallers of Saint John of God (better known in Old Regime France as the Frères de Charité) was founded in Spain in the mid-16th century by Saint John of God. The order spread to France in the 17th and 18th centuries. By 1789 there were 355 Frères serving in 39 hospitals in France and its colonies, with a total of about 5,000 beds under their care.

"In Defence of Sugar: The Logistics of Fortifying the French Antilles under the Regency, 1715–1723." Proceedings of the Meeting of the French Colonial Historical Society 19 (1994): 68–86. https://www.jstor.org/stable/43007765. Régent, Frédéric. "Approche démographique de la population servile en Guadeloupe à la fin de l'ancien Régime." Annales de démographie historique 119, no. 1 (2010): 5–25. Lafleur, Gérard. Note: The author of the 1782 report "État du Clergé du département de la Marine" includes a note that the "Hôpital de la Guadeloupe," run by the Brothers of Charity, received 2,180 livres per year from the king. The letters patent of February 1686, which permitted the Brothers to settle in Martinique, also authorized them to establish themselves in Guadeloupe. They enjoyed the same privileges as other religious orders and owned one of the "finest plantations on the island." The same author wrote that less was known about the hospitals in Martinique; he also did not know the number of enslaved people who resided on the Brothers' plantations. According to research conducted by Fred J. Thorpe, the Brothers of Charity already had more than 250 slaves and an income of around 60,000 livres across their properties in Guadeloupe by 1721. According to Frédéric Régent, a notarial entry from October 1792 indicates that in at least one location (the Dolé plantation), men and women appeared to live in separate spaces: 348 slaves were recorded as distributed among 26 "cases à nègres." The inventory of the property belonging to the Brothers of Charity recorded the deaths of 20 out of 376 slaves living on the estate between November 7, 1791, and October 19, 1792. Altogether, the Frères de la Charité possessed 411 slaves in 1789 in Guadeloupe, with a collective recorded value of 822,000 livres, according to historian Gérard Lafleur.

Reference: "Inventaire des biens du collège (25 septembre 1784)." Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer, F5 A 18. Aix-en-Provence. Rennard, Joseph. "Les écoles à la Martinique au XVIIe et au XVIIIe siècle." Martinique 4, no. 4 (1944): 200–206.
Note: The College St. Victor, in Fort Royal (Fort-de-France), founded at the behest of Father Charles-François de Coutances, head of the Capuchin Mission in Martinique, was served by the Brothers of the Christian Schools from 1782. A 1784 inventory of the college's assets included a plantation with 21 enslaved individuals (Charles, Zy, Papillon, Jean, Vaillant, Joseph, Zorelle, Francisque, Lafleur, Benoit, Thibagne, Marie Roze, Marie Anne, Nicole, Celimene, Marie Elizabeth, Jean Louis, Jean Marie, Celeste, Tandée, and Marie Françoise).It is interesting to note that Father Coutances also founded, around the same time, a school for girls in the city under the name La Providence. However, it was not run by nuns who had taken vows, but by unmarried or single women belonging to a non-affiliated educational establishment: "les demoiselles formées dans la Maison de Saint-Etienne de la Rochelle et affiliées à cette Maison."It is notable that Father Charles-François specifically recommended that no cloistered community of women religious be permitted in slave colonies, as any semblance that white persons were imprisoned would potentially lead to insubordination among the enslaved. Saint-Victor was located along Sainte Elizabeth street, in the area of the current courthouse and the former town hall (now Théâtre Aimé Césaire).

Reference: Trudel, Marcel. Canada's Forgotten Slaves: Two Hundred Years of Bondage. Translated by George Tombs. Montréal: Véhicule Press, 2013.
Note: Thee Jesuits at their mission at Michilimackinac possessed four Native slaves.

Reference: Trudel, Marcel. "L'attitude de l'Église catholique vis-à-vis l'esclavage au Canada français." Report of the Annual Meeting / Rapports annuels de la Société historique du Canada 40, no. 1 (1961): 28–34.
Note: Historian Marcel Trudel writes that the three Native servants recorded as being in the employ of the Jesuits at Quebec had all been enslaved. In 1720, upon the invitation of the intendant of New France, Michel Bégon, various religious communities submitted requests to be given some of a cargo of one hundred enslaved Africans shipped to the colony by the French Company of the Indies. Various religious communities obtained slaves, including the Jesuits at Quebec City, at Saint-François-du-Lac (or Odanak, a village for Abenaki converts), at Sault-Saint-Louis (or Kahnawake, a village for Iroquois converts), as well as at their mission stations in Detroit and Michilimackinac; the Frères de la charité, who operated a military hospital at the fortified town of Louisbourg; the Hôpital Général in Quebec; the Hôtel-Dieu hospital in Montreal; the Congregation of Notre Dame; and the Hôpital Général of Montreal, where Grey Nuns (Order of Sisters of Charity) foundress, the widow Marie-Marguerite d'Youville, had already brought the slaves of her late husband. After the conquest of Canada by the British, several of those who returned to France left their slaves to the Hospital. Furthermore, several bishops of Quebec owned slaves. Historian Marcel Trudel has uncovered records of four bishops of Quebec who owned slaves during their episcopate: Saint-Vallier (whose term lasted from 1687 to 1727) and Pontbriand (1741-1760) each owned at least one enslaved Native servant; Dosquet (1733-1739) and Plessis (1806-1825) each owned at least one enslaved servant described as black.  Secular priests across New France also owned slaves. One of these was among the last individuals to trade in slaves in Canadian history when, in 1796, he sold an enslaved woman of African descent named Rose. There were only two more recorded slave sales in Canada—all by 1800. In 1834, slavery was outlawed in Canada (as part of the British Empire).

Reference: Dial, Andrew. "Antoine Lavalette, Slave Murderer: A Forgotten Scandal of the French West Indies." Journal of Jesuit Studies 8, no. 1 (2020): 37–55. Villiers du Terrage, Marc. Les dernières années de la Louisiane française: Le chevalier de Kerlérec, d'Abbadie, Aubry, Laussat. Paris: Guilmoto, 1904.
Note: On July 9, 1764, the Superior Council of Louisiana made the decision to expel the Jesuits from the colony. This action followed broader movements against the Jesuit Order throughout the French empire, where they were seen as a threat to royal authority and public order. Interesting, in addition to repeating some of the justifications used by the Parlements of France--that the Jesuits attacked royal authority, the authority of the bishops, and "the public peace"--the Council accused the Jesuits of neglecting their missionary duties to focus on their wealth and plantations, stating that the Jesuits "n'avaient eu aucun soin de leurs missions; qu'ils n'avaient pensé qu'à faire valoir leurs habitations" (they had taken no care of their missions and only thought of enhancing the value of their estates). Additionally, they were charged with usurping the vicar general's authority in New Orleans. Their chapels were ordered to be razed, and the Jesuits were forced to return to France, with their assets seized and chapel ornaments transferred to the Capuchins. This marked the end of the Jesuits' presence in Louisiana, reflecting wider anti-Jesuit sentiments in the French world.

Reference: Carayon, Auguste. Bannissement des jésuites de la Louisiane; relation et lettres inédites. Paris: L'Ecureux, 1865. "Some Points on the Jesuit Mission in the Illinois." In Clarence Alvord and Clarence Carter, eds., The New Regime, 1765–1767. Collections of the Illinois State Historical Library, vol. 11. Springfield, 1916, 326–28. Cited in Dial, Andrew. "Antoine Lavalette, Slave Murderer: A Forgotten Scandal of the French West Indies." Journal of Jesuit Studies 8, no. 1 (2020): 37–55.
Note: According to Carayon, in 1764, the Jesuits of Kaskaskia, in the Illinois Country (Upper Louisiana), possessed 48 slaves of both West African and Amerindian origin when they returned to France in the wake of the suppression of the French provinces of the Society of Jesus by King Louis XV. According to a 1766 letter, there were 68 enslaved persons living on the estate.

Reference: Dial, Andrew. "Antoine Lavalette, Slave Murderer: A Forgotten Scandal of the French West Indies." Journal of Jesuit Studies 8, no. 1 (2020): 37–55. Villiers du Terrage, Marc. Note: The Jesuits established themselves in Saint-Pierre, Martinique, in 1640, building the first stone structure in the city at the Habitation Perrinelle. Their presence was central to the religious and educational life of the colony, with the construction of a church and the management of a sugar plantation. The Jesuits also played a key role in shaping the religious landscape by overseeing the parish of Saint-Pierre. However, following their expulsion from the island in 1763, their land was sold, marking the end of their direct influence in Martinique; Stephan Lenik, “Mission Plantations, Space, and Social Control: Jesuits as Planters in French Caribbean Colonies and Frontiers,” Journal of Social Archaeology 12, no. 1 (February 1, 2012): 51–71.

Note: In 1762, the cash-strapped Jesuits of the Iles du Vent sold their vast sugar plantation of Bisdary to a private buyer, just as the English occupation of the island was ending and just before the suppression of the Crown's order to expel the order from the French Antilles and liquidate its properties. According to records of the sale, 312 enslaved men, women, and children lived on the plantation.

Note: In 1762, the Jesuits’ properties in French Guiana were inventoried ahead of the liquidation of the order’s assets in the French empire. Yannick Le Roux estimates that a total of between 1,000 and 1,500 persons labored on their plantations ("Loyola," "Mont-Louis," plus the Oyapoc mission). Their plantations produced half of all the cacao and coffee exported from the colony. Exact figures exist for 509.

Note: Jesuits were the single largest slaveholder on that island. Controversially, their business manager in the Antilles had also negotiated the purchase of 200-300 slaves for sugar production on the small satellite colony. When they were taken to court for unpaid debts related to their dealing in slaves, their properties in the region were ordered liquidated. Nevertheless each priest was allowed to retain one enslaved man, one enslaved woman, and a child as “domestics.”

Reference: Trudel, Marcel. Canada's Forgotten Slaves: Two Hundred Years of Bondage. Translated by George Tombs. Montréal: Véhicule Press, 2013.
Note: The Jesuits at Saint-François-du-Lac (or Odanak, a village for Abenaki converts),  were served by an enslaved Sioux woman named Françoise whom the priests described as “quite pretty [with] a sweet and seductive voice.”

Reference: Trudel, Marcel. Canada's Forgotten Slaves: Two Hundred Years of Bondage. Translated by George Tombs. Montréal: Véhicule Press, 2013. Trudel, Marcel. "L'attitude de l'Église catholique vis-à-vis l'esclavage au Canada français." Report of the Annual Meeting / Rapports annuels de la Société historique du Canada 40, no. 1 (1961): 28–34.
Note: In 1720, upon the invitation of the intendant of New France, Michel Bégon, various religious communities submitted requests to be given some of a cargo of one hundred enslaved Africans shipped to the colony by the French Company of the Indies. Various religious communities obtained slaves, including the Jesuits at Quebec City, at Saint-François-du-Lac (or Odanak, a village for Abenaki converts), at Sault-Saint-Louis (or Kahnawake, a village for Iroquois converts), as well as at their mission stations in Detroit and Michilimackinac; the Frères de la charité, who operated a military hospital at the fortified town of Louisbourg; the Hôpital Général in Quebec; the Hôtel-Dieu hospital in Montreal; the Congregation of Notre Dame; and the Hôpital Général of Montreal, where Grey Nuns (Order of Sisters of Charity) foundress, the widow Marie-Marguerite d'Youville, had already brought the slaves of her late husband. After the conquest of Canada by the British, several of those who returned to France left their slaves to the Hospital. Furthermore, several bishops of Quebec owned slaves. Historian Marcel Trudel has uncovered records of four bishops of Quebec who owned slaves during their episcopate: Saint-Vallier (whose term lasted from 1687 to 1727) and Pontbriand (1741-1760) each owned at least one enslaved Native servant; Dosquet (1733-1739) and Plessis (1806-1825) each owned at least one enslaved servant described as black.  Secular priests across New France also owned slaves. One of these was among the last individuals to trade in slaves in Canadian history when, in 1796, he sold an enslaved woman of African descent named Rose. There were only two more recorded slave sales in Canada—all by 1800. In 1834, slavery was outlawed in Canada (as part of the British Empire).

Reference: Trudel, Marcel. Canada's Forgotten Slaves: Two Hundred Years of Bondage. Translated by George Tombs. Montréal: Véhicule Press, 2013. Dilhet, Jean. Note: A letter by a Lazarist brother provides a detailed description of the parish plantations of Ste. Marie (Isle de Bourbon) in the 1740s. The rectory was a well-designed wooden house, within a picturesque courtyard featuring a garden with tamarind arbors, storage buildings, a henyard, and an outdoor kitchen. The estate had significant agricultural and livestock assets, such as poultry, pigs, cows, horses, crops, and a coffee plantation with 6,000 plants. The garden and plantation were worked by "five households" of enslaved people, illustrating the missionaries' reliance on the slave labor provided by the French East India Company for their plantations' operations. Brother Pierre Niox has developed a second estate in the same parish into a well-ordered plantation.The letter also mentions the transportation of coffee and wood to St. Denis (the colonial capital) and Ile de France (the neighboring island), facilitated by a team of horses called the Diligence. The missionaries' ecclesiastical council frequently met at the Ste. Marie estate.

Reference: "Recensement général de l'isle de Bourbon," 1787. Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer, G1/480. Aix-en-Provence. Letter from Brother Etienne Lecocq, ca. 1740. Archives Nationales (site du Caran), M/214, dossier 9, pièce 4.
Note: A letter by a Lazarist coadjutor-brother, who served as the manager of the Lazarist plantation at St. Paul (Isle de Bourbon), provides a detailed description of their properties in that parish in the 1740s. These included well-maintained gardens producing vegetables and fruits. The church was built on a small hill overlooking the district, near a courtyard bordered with laurels. A nearby stream provided fresh water and irrigated the gardens. Next to the church was a presbytery for the missionaries, with a yard bordered by tamarind trees, which served as a venue for teaching catechism to enslaved people both morning and night. It was also in St. Paul that the missionaries taught "7 or 8 young black children" to read. The plantation, located above the town called "La Montagne," was worked by "eight households" of enslaved people. The Lazarist brother oversaw the plantation and transported crops down to the town with the help of two horses.

The presbytery, located near fertile land, supported livestock and produced abundant crops like wheat, rice, and millet. Religious services took place in a well-decorated chapel built by a local inhabitant, half a league from the presbytery.

Port-Louis: Diocèse de Port-Louis, 1972, 30–31. "Plan général de l'Isle de France," after 1772. Bibliothèque nationale de France, département Cartes et plans, GE SH 18 PF 219 DIV 2 P 24. https://gallica.bnf.fr/ark:/12148/btv1b53105272r. "Carte particulière de l'isle de France," 1772. Bibliothèque nationale de France, département Cartes et plans, GE C-9307. https://gallica.bnf.fr/ark:/12148/btv1b530986308.
Note: The Lazarists (AKA Congregation of the Mission), founded in Paris by Vincent de Paul and recruited by the French East India Company to serve the parishes of the Mascarene Islands, managed upwards of 613 “parish slaves” producing coffee, cotton, and staple crops (187 on Île de France [Mauritius] in 1780; 436 on Bourbon Island [Réunion] in 1787). In 1723, the Port Louis parish was allocated 156 arpents along the Rivière des Lataniers, near Pieter Both (mountain), later known as "the Vallée-des-Prêtres," for developing a plantation. Two maps from the late 1770s show this property as belonging to “Les Prestres” and suggest it was the most developed in the valley. A 1772 map marks it as Plot 313 and depicts cleared land, one red slash representing a large house, and nine smaller marks likely representing outbuildings that housed enslaved workers (more than any other property in the area). In 1772, the mother parish received an additional 34 arpents in "l'enfoncement Courtois" at Pailles. All parish properties were confiscated during the Revolution and later sold under Napoleon by General Decaen, the last French Governor. Only the Vallée-des-Prêtres remained in the hands of the missionaries.
RefRel: Mamet, Joseph. Les Lazaristes, fondateurs de la chrétienté de l’Ile de France. Port Louis: Diocèse de Port-Louis, 1972.

Reference: Alvord, Clarence Walworth. Kaskaskia Records, 1778–1790. Springfield: Trustees of the Illinois State Historical Library, 1909. Trudel, Marcel. Canada's Forgotten Slaves: Two Hundred Years of Bondage. Translated by George Tombs. Montréal: Véhicule Press, 2013. "Mission des Cahoquias & Son Etat," January 1, 1732. Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer, G1/464 (microfilm: 5Mi1240, Archives nationales, CARAN, Paris).
Note: The Séminaire de Québec, which was not an order but a community of secular priests active as missionaries throughout French North America, indicated on a deed of sale from 1763 that they owned 30 African or African-descended and Native slaves at their mission station Sainte-Famille. Trudel misidentifies this as “Kaskaskia,” another post in the Illinois country. An earlier inventory of the "Mission des Cahoquias" from 1723 listed several missionaries and 8 slaves (3 men and 1 woman of African descent, described as "Nègres," and 3 men and 1 woman of indigenous background, described as "Esclaves Indiens"), in addition to a barn and livestock and "35 terres en valeur."After the American War of Independence and the annexation of the Northwest Territory by the US, the remaining priests of the Seminary returned to Quebec in the 1780s, but left behind a number of enslaved men and women or sold them on dubious legal grounds. In 1787, the parishioners of Cahokia sent a letter to the priests of the Seminary of Quebec, requesting that they relinquish all property in the area for use by the parish vestry-board (fabrique) of Holy Family parish. They hoped, in particular, to recover ownership of “some families of negroes on the Spanish side who are of considerable value…and were either given their liberty or were sold by M. Forget, the last vicar in the region during French colonial times, without being authorized to do it. There are even some here in the parish of Cahokia… we limit ourselves to praying you, gentlemen, kindly to interest yourselves to cause these negroes to re-enter into the power of our mission. These have greatly increased by propagation.” (Letter, Cahokia, June 6, 1787)

As the Récollets (French Franciscans) could not in principle own property, neither individually nor collectively, it is likely that these three bondpeople were donated to the parish.

Province: The Sisters of Charity of Montreal
Reference: Trudel, Marcel. Canada's Forgotten Slaves: Two Hundred Years of Bondage. Translated by George Tombs. Montréal: Véhicule Press, 2013.
Note: The Hôpital Général of Montreal, where the Grey Nuns (Order of Sisters of Charity) were founded by the widow Marie-Marguerite d'Youville, received donations of enslaved people. Marie-Marguerite d'Youville brought the enslaved people of her late husband to the hospital. After the British conquest of Canada, several French individuals who returned to France left their slaves to the hospital. By 1774(?), at least three enslaved people (all indigenous) had been donated to the hospital; an additional three to four enslaved people belonged to Mother d'Youville.

Reference: Varachaud, Marie-Christine, and André Zysberg. "Spiritains et la 'France équinoxiale': la mission de Guyane (vers 1770–1789)." Mémoire Spiritaine 17, no. 17 (2003): 53. https://dsc.duq.edu/memoire-spiritaine/vol17/iss17/7.
Note: The government chose priests trained at the Séminaire du Saint-Esprit in Paris to replace the Jesuits in Guyane. Donations to the Jesuit-founded college in Cayenne and assets from the Guyana mission were combined with royal funds to purchase a large estate in Aprouague. Swiss engineer Guisan from Surinam, an expert in land reclamation, transformed the estate into a plantation equipped for cultivating sugarcane, manufacturing sugar, and distilling rum. By 1787, the plantation was nearly complete and employed 221 slaves. Despite estimates of generating up to 100,000 livres per year in net revenue, this goal had not yet been achieved.

Reference: Varachaud, Marie-Christine, and André Zysberg. "Spiritains et la 'France équinoxiale': la mission de Guyane (vers 1770–1789)." Mémoire Spiritaine 17, no. 17 (2003). https://dsc.duq.edu/memoire-spiritaine/vol17/iss17/7.
Note: The Spiritans took over the administration of the college founded in the late 17th century by the Jesuits (now known as Collège Eugène Nonnon)

Reference: "Tessier's Epoque" and "Tessier's Diary." Associated Archives of St. Mary's Seminary and University. Baltimore, MD. Ulshafer, Thomas R. "Slavery and the Early Sulpician Community in Maryland." U.S. Catholic Historian 37, no. 2 (2019): 1–21. https://doi.org/10.1353/cht.2019.0013.
Note: A small community of priests from the Society of Saint Sulpice arrived in Baltimore in 1791 as émigrés from France, invited by the United States' first bishop, John Carroll, to establish the country’s first institution for the training of Catholic clergy. They also built a small boarding school for boys on the campus: St. Mary’s College. The chapel became the spiritual home of the local Catholic community, including numerous refugees from the Haitian Revolution, among them men and women of color, such as Mary Lange, who would go on to begin the first congregation of nuns of African descent in the US, the Oblate Sisters of Providence, in 1829. Between 1796 and 1822, the names of at least ten domestics working on the site of the Sulpicians’ seminary and college can be found in the pages of the diary of the community’s superior, Father Tessier. Sulpician historian Thomas Ulshafer finds evidence that at least six of them were enslaved (Jack, Peg, Nicholas, Hortense, an unnamed man, and an unnamed woman). Most were hired out from people in the Baltimore area (many of whom were émigrés from France or Saint-Domingue) or sent from Bohemia Plantation (property of the former Jesuit province of Maryland), which was under the management of the Sulpicians beginning in 1793. Ulshafer suggests that the Maryland community of Sulpicians did not have sufficient funds in the 1790s for the outright purchase of enslaved people. The Sulpicians also hired a number of free white and black people in the city.

Reference: Keller-Lapp, Heidi. "Floating Cloisters and Heroic Women: French Ursuline Missionaries, 1639–1744." World History Connected 4, no. 3 (2007). https://worldhistoryconnected.press.uillinois.edu/4.3/lapp.html. "Lettres patentes portant confirmation de l'établissement des Ursulines à la Martinique," August 28, 1681. Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer, C8 B 19 N° 37. Aix-en-Provence.
Note: In the late seventeenth century, the Ursulines of France aimed to prove their suitability for a mission in Martinique by highlighting their fervor, youth, health, and strong constitutions. They benefitted from their reputation for hardiness and zeal in ministering to Native people and French settlers in New France, as emphasized in letters from mother superiors in Rouen and St. Denis. A community from the latter house was eventually chosen. The first contingent arrived in Martinique in 1682 and immediately began educating the daughters of French and Creole colonists as well as enslaved African girls.Changes in state and colonial policies under Louis XV's Minister of the Navy, Jean-Baptiste Colbert, meant their mission would no longer involve the conversion of indigenous people. Instead, they focused on educating the colonists' daughters to be good French Catholic mothers. This shift aligned with a broader policy of social policing within the colony. Jesuit sponsor Fr. Channevelle envisioned these girls instructing their own children, domestic workers, and slaves to be dutiful and devout.As state resources dwindled and financial support from France decreased due to fragmented patron networks, declining monasticism, and competing state projects, the Ursulines' missions became financially unstable. They increasingly negotiated with colonial officials to secure support, emphasizing their role in teaching and upholding French culture over traditional missionary work. Archival records show their poverty and the shift in focus towards social utility. Yet, their teaching apostolate and missionary zeal continued to make them attractive candidates for other mission fields, including in Louisiana.Nevertheless, the letters that established their presence on the island tried to provide for their long-term sustainability by ensuring their right to generate revenues from sugar plantations and slave labor (indeed, it was more or less a stipulation of their contract). Two pious local women made donations of plantations to be held by the nuns directly for their support. They would be under the patronage of the Compagnie des Indes occidentales; the letters were signed by the king and his minister, Colbert.In exchange for being exempt from paying the head tax (capitation) and corvée labor imposition on their enslaved property, "up to the number of 200 workers," they agreed to make yearly payments of sugar produced at their estates in the quantity of "quatre cent milliers par chacun an." As was the case with all other religious entities whose lettres patentes also stipulated tax-free status on enslaved persons up to a certain number, we should assume that the enslaved property possessed by the nuns in Martinique eventually exceeded 200.In 1751, a chapel was built within the Ursulines Convent site in St. Pierre, with the first stone laid by Governor Maximin de Bompar. During the French Revolution in 1792, the Ursuline convent was declared national property and became the meeting place for the oath-taking priests of the Patriotic Society of Saint-Pierre. The English occupation of Martinique from 1794 to 1802 allowed the Ursulines to reclaim their property. The land where the chapel (later the parish church of Saint-Etienne-du-Centre) and the Ursuline convent once stood before the devastating eruption of the volcano Pelée in 1902 is now occupied by the Gabriel Suvélor Stadium.

Reference: "Règlement du Conseil supérieur de Pondichéry pour l'établissement des dames religieuses Ursulines... destinées pour l'éducation des jeunes filles des François établis aux Indes." Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer, F5 A 40, fols. 27–59. Aix-en-Provence.
Note: A contract signed between the French East India Company’s Superior Council in Pondicherry and the Ursuline Sisters of Vannes on 29 November 1738, made provisions for the Company to procure “two pions (foot servants) and two gardeners.” These may have been enslaved individuals, perhaps from among the topa community of Portuguese-speaking Indian (and mixed-race) enslaved persons and their descendants in the region. An amendment to the contract imposed by the local bishop, based in San Thomé (Chennai) stipulated that the nuns learn Portuguese in order to teach “the Slaves the catechism [by] speaking to them through the grill” of the convent gate.

Reference: Trudel, Marcel. Dictionnaire des esclaves et de leurs propriétaires au Canada français. Québec: Hurtubise HMH, 1990.
Note: Along the St. Lawrence River, there are indications that religious orders in Quebec and Montreal held 20 African and/or indigenous slaves around mid-century, and the Ursulines in particular, based at Trois-Rivières, held 5.
